meta.title: Autosave overwrites the latest draft
meta.summary: Rapid edits plus flaky network produce out-of-order autosave acknowledgements that replace newer text with stale content.
signals: Textarea jumps back to older content

context.symptom: A user types a paragraph, pauses briefly, continues typing, then sees the textarea revert to an older partial draft once the network catches up.
context.userImpact: Users stop trusting autosave because it can delete or overwrite their most recent work.
context.environment: React form with autosave every few seconds, optimistic local state, and a server endpoint that returns the saved draft payload.

evidence.title: Request sequence
evidence.body: Draft version 12 is sent after version 11, but version 11 resolves later and still replaces the editor state.

evidence.title: Current success path
Product behavior
evidence.body: Offline retry plus quick edits reproduces the overwrite almost every time.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What is the main failure mode?
stages.helperText: Focus on why old data is allowed to become source of truth again.

options.label: Autosave acknowledgements are applied without version checks, so stale responses can overwrite newer local edits.
options.feedback: Correct. The broken part is commit logic around out-of-order save responses.

options.label: The textarea updates too quickly for the autosave loop to keep up.
options.feedback: Typing speed is not the core issue. The app lacks conflict handling for async saves.

options.label: The backend is slow because the save endpoint needs more memory.
options.feedback: Backend latency can trigger the timing, but it does not justify applying stale responses.

options.label: The client should always replace local state with the latest server payload, regardless of local edits.
options.feedback: That policy is exactly what makes stale acknowledgements destructive here.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: Rank the three most useful debug steps first.
stages.helperText: Start by proving the ordering and commit mismatch, not by redesigning the full editor.

candidates.label: Log local draft version, request id, and response id for each autosave cycle.
Reproduce with slow or flaky network while continuing to edit during in-flight saves.
Inspect when the UI clears dirty state and whether stale acknowledgements can still commit.
Raise the autosave interval first so requests overlap less often.
Disable local typing updates until every save completes.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in a robust autosave fix?
stages.helperText: Select the options that preserve local intent when responses arrive out of order.

options.label: Send a version token or draft revision and ignore acknowledgements that no longer match the current revision.
options.feedback: Correct. Save responses need a freshness contract before they can update state.

options.label: Clear dirty state only when the acknowledgement matches the current local draft revision.
options.feedback: Correct. This prevents the UI from falsely claiming the latest text is safely persisted.

options.label: Abort or supersede older in-flight saves when a newer draft replaces them.
options.feedback: Correct. This reduces overlap and supports a take-latest autosave policy.

options.label: Use the local wall-clock timestamp only and assume whichever timestamp is larger is correct.
options.feedback: Timestamps can help, but alone they are weaker than explicit draft versions and commit guards.

options.label: Reset the textarea from the server payload after every successful save, even if the user kept typing.
options.feedback: That is the destructive behavior you are trying to remove.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the strongest follow-up guardrail?
stages.helperText: Pick the guard that proves stale saves cannot overwrite the newest draft anymore.

options.label: Add an out-of-order autosave integration test and surface a conflict-safe status signal in the UI.
options.feedback: Correct. You want both a regression test and a product-level signal that the latest draft is or is not safely persisted.

options.label: Increase the autosave interval so overlapping saves happen less often.
options.feedback: That reduces frequency but does not prove correctness when overlap still happens.

options.label: Ask QA to write a long paragraph manually before release and trust the outcome.
options.feedback: Manual coverage is not strong enough for timing-sensitive overwrite bugs.

options.label: Turn autosave off entirely and require a manual save button.
options.feedback: That removes the feature instead of fixing ordering and persistence guarantees.

scoreBands.label: Draft consistency still fragile
scoreBands.summary: You saw the overwrite, but not the freshness contract needed between local draft state and save acknowledgements.

scoreBands.label: Correct direction, weak guardrail
scoreBands.summary: You identified stale acknowledgements, but one part of the fix or regression plan still relied on hope.

scoreBands.label: State-consistency thinking
scoreBands.summary: You protected local intent with versions, commit guards, and a test that reproduces the real race.

idealRunbook: Log draft revisions and request order.
Reproduce overlapping saves while the user keeps editing.
Guard commits with explicit revision matching.

teachingBlocks.title: Autosave rule of thumb
teachingBlocks.text: Never let the server payload blindly replace local text unless you know it still corresponds to the current draft.

teachingBlocks.title: Strong answer signals
items: Dirty-state rules tied to the current revision

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: What product signal would you show if the latest draft is only partially synced?
